elements.sent-2411-001: Leveraging the flexible expressive ability of (Max)SMT and the powerful solving ability of SMT solvers, we propose a novel layout model named SMT-Layout.
elements.sent-2411-002: SMT-Layout is the first constraint-based layout model that can support real-time interaction for real-world GUI layout adapting to various screen sizes with only one specification.
elements.sent-2411-003: Previous works neglect the hierarchy information among widgets and thus cannot exploit the reasoning ability of solvers.
elements.sent-2411-004: For the first time, we introduce Boolean variables to encode the hierarchy relationship, boosting the reasoning ability of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-005: The workflow is divided into two stages.
elements.sent-2411-006: At the development end, two novel preprocessing methods are proposed to simplify constraints and extract useful information in advance, easing the solving burden.
elements.sent-2411-007: After deploying constraints to the terminal end, SMT solvers are applied to solve constraints incrementally.
elements.sent-2411-008: Besides mainstream SMT solvers, a local search solver is customized to this scenario.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experiments show that SMT-Layout can support millisecond-level interaction for real-world layouts, even on devices with low computing power and rigorous memory limitations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Compared to conventional graphical user interface (GUI) layout models such as grid and group, constraint-based layout models are more powerful and flexible: they can align widgets across the hierarchy~\cite{lutteroth2008modular and are easier to maintain~\cite{lutteroth2006user,zeidler2012comparing , and they have been widely applied in GUI design.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, Apple's AutoLayout~\cite{sadun2013ios adapts interfaces on different devices.}
elements.sent-2411-012: CSS's Flex Box uses constraints to fit the content and solve alignment problems~\cite{weyl2017flexbox .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The early typical constraint-based layout models are based on linear constraint systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: The specifications of layout are modeled as linear equalities and inequalities, and solved by linear or quadratic programming solvers ~\cite{badros2001cassowary, bill1992bricklayer, borning1997solving, hosobe2000scalable, lutteroth2008domain .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, constraint-based layout models share the same drawback as conventional layout models: when the layouts need to adapt to screens with drastically different sizes, multiple specifications need to be defined and synchronized, which can be time-consuming and can cause maintenance difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-016: In 2019, a constraint-based layout model called ORCLayout was proposed to specify layout based on OR constraints~\cite{jiang2019orc , allowing the GUI layout to adapt to multiple screen sizes with only one specification.}
elements.sent-2411-017: An OR constraint is a disjunction of constraints where only one needs to be true, and soft constraints with weights are introduced to express the preference of different terms in an OR constraint.
elements.sent-2411-018: In essence, ORCLayout models the layout problem as an optimized version of {\it Satisfiability Modolo Theories (SMT) , also known as MaxSMT.}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, state-of-the-art (Max)SMT solvers such as Z3 perform poorly on ORCLayout constraints, mainly because ORCLayout cannot model the hierarchy relationships among widgets with Boolean constraints, and thus the powerful reasoning ability of SMT solvers cannot be utilized to reduce the exponent combination of OR constraints.
elements.sent-2411-020: A solver called ORCSolver is proposed to solve the MaxSMT problem, based on a branch-and-bound (B\&B) approach with modular heuristics dedicated to specific layout patterns~\cite{jiang2020orcsolver , which is not a general method and does not support incremental SMT solving.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Overall, ORCLayout does not leverage the main power of SMT solvers including reasoning and incremental solving, and thus it cannot achieve real-time interaction.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, ORCLayout cannot handle real-world layout design since it does not fully utilize the expression ability of (Max)SMT.
elements.sent-2411-023: Specifically, it does not provide abundant layout patterns supporting practical design.
elements.sent-2411-024: As summarized in Table~\ref{layout_cmp , the conventional layout model and linear constraint-based layout model can support real-time interaction for real-world layouts, and they have been widely applied in practical GUI design.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, they need to maintain multiple specifications for various screen sizes, which can be time-consuming and may cause consistency issues.
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, ORCLayout can adapt to multiple screen sizes with one unique specification, but it is not suitable for practical GUI design because it cannot handle real-world layout and cannot support real-time response.
elements.sent-2411-027: As far as we know, no layout model can support real-time interaction for real-world GUI layouts adapting various screen sizes with only one specification.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this paper, by fully utilizing the flexible expression ability of (Max)SMT and the powerful solving ability of SMT solvers, we aim to propose a practical constraint-based layout model named SMT-Layout to satisfy all dimensions in Table~\ref{layout_cmp .}

elements.sent-2411-029: We propose a practical modeling module based on the flexible expression ability of MaxSMT: abundant preset layout containers are provided for designers to flexibly specify real-world layouts and convert them to MaxSMT constraints.
elements.sent-2411-030: A special layout container named ``Placeholder'' is proposed to accommodate alternative layouts for varying screen sizes with only one specification.
elements.sent-2411-031: We consider the solving efficiency to be the main bottleneck since solving the layout specifications in the form of MaxSMT is NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-032: As shown below, the main effort is devoted to achieving real-time interaction as the screen size varies, leveraging the reasoning and incremental solving ability of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-033: Note that our approaches are general and do not rely on specific layout patterns, so they can adapt to new layout patterns without special customization.
elements.sent-2411-034: This work introduces Boolean variables to illustrate the visibility property of widgets, and for the first time, directly specifies the hierarchy relationship among widgets by the Boolean constraints on their visibility property.
elements.sent-2411-035: Based on these Boolean constraints, the reasoning ability of the SMT solver can be fully utilized to infer invisible widgets and omit their corresponding constraints, which can drastically simplify the original formula, thus boosting the solving efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-036: Soft constraints are also introduced as Boolean variables with corresponding weights to express the preference.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose to ease the solving burden by simplifying the formulas and extracting useful information in advance.
elements.sent-2411-038: Specifically, a two-stage architecture is proposed to divide the workflow into the {\it development end and the {\it terminal end .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The layout is specified and converted to formulas at the development end, and the converted formulas are then deployed to the terminal end for solving and display.
elements.sent-2411-040: At the development end, two novel preprocessing methods are proposed to enhance the solving efficiency: First, the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening method converts the MaxSMT formula to SMT formulas by predetermining the assignments of soft constraints corresponding to the interval of the size property.}
elements.sent-2411-041: With these predetermined Boolean variables, we can apply the reasoning ability of SMT solvers to infer the visibility property of widgets, based on the Boolean constraints w.r.t.. the hierarchy relationship.
elements.sent-2411-042: The corresponding constraints regarding those invisible widgets can be omitted, significantly reducing the search space.
elements.sent-2411-043: Second, the {\it Independent widget extraction method utilizes the modular nature of layout designing to extract independent widgets, and the original formula is divided into multiple independent formulas with moderate scales, which are easier to solve.}
elements.sent-2411-044: After the preprocessing, the preview module evaluates the layout in advance and prompts the designers about potential issues.
elements.sent-2411-045: At the terminal end, a two-level solving strategy is proposed to solve these constraints, leveraging the reasoning and incremental solving power of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-046: Besides the mainstream complete SMT solver such as Z3~\cite{moura2008z3 , a lightweight local search SMT solver called LocalSMT is customized as the backend solver.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Local search is appropriate for the incremental scenario, since most constraints remain satisfied when resizing the screen, and local search can be applied to efficiently find a new solution near the current one.
elements.sent-2411-048: Moreover, LocalSMT has a compact data structure and can save memory usage, which is suitable for the terminal end with rigorous memory limitations.
elements.sent-2411-049: Overall, we apply the main power of (Max)SMT to design and implement a novel constraint-based layout model called SMT-Layout, based on the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-050: Based on the expression ability of MaxSMT, a novel modeling module is proposed to specify the GUI layout.
elements.sent-2411-051: Abundant layout containers are provided to support real-world layouts adapting to various screen sizes with only one specification (Sect.~\ref{preset container ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Boolean variables are introduced to illustrate the visibility property of widgets, based on which the hierarchical relationships are directly specified with Boolean constraints, boosting the reasoning ability of SMT solvers (Sect.~\ref{boolean hierarchy relation ).}
elements.sent-2411-053: A two-stage workflow architecture is proposed to ease the solving burden at the terminal end (Sect.~\ref{archi ).}
elements.sent-2411-054: Specifically, at the development end, two novel preprocessing methods, namely {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening and {\it Independent widget extraction , are proposed to simplify the formulas and extract useful information in advance (Sect.~\ref{preprocess module ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: A two-level solving strategy is proposed to solve the constraints based on the reasoning and incremental solving ability of SMT solvers (Sect.~\ref{two-level ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: Besides mainstream complete SMT solvers, a local search SMT solver is customized for the incremental scenario as the backend solver, enhancing the solving efficiency and saving memory usage (Sect.~\ref{sls for layout ).}
elements.sent-2411-057: Based on SMT-Layout, we have specified 12 layouts derived from real-world GUIs, and each of them can adapt to various screen sizes with only one specification.
elements.sent-2411-058: The experimental results show that SMT-Layout can achieve millisecond-level real-time responsiveness in handling these layout specifications, even on devices with constrained computational resources, such as mobile phones.
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, the memory overhead is minimal with local search solvers serving as the backend solver (Sect.~\ref{evaluation ).}
elements.sent-2411-060: As an important fragment of the formal method, Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) decides the satisfiability of a first-order logic formula concerning certain background theories.
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, in the context of constraint-based layout, the background theory is the linear arithmetic theory, consisting of arithmetic formulae in the form of linear equalities or inequalities over arithmetic variables ($\sum_{i=0 ^n{a_ix_i\leq k}$ or $\sum_{i=0 ^n{a_ix_i = k}$, where $a_i$ are coefficients and $x_i$ are arithmetic variables).}}
elements.sent-2411-062: An atomic formula can be a propositional variable or an arithmetic formula.
elements.sent-2411-063: A $literal$ is an atomic formula, or the negation of an atomic formula.
elements.sent-2411-064: A $clause$ is the disjunction of a set of literals, and a formula in {\it conjunctive normal form (CNF) is the conjunction of a set of clauses.}
elements.sent-2411-065: A $clause$ consisting of only one literal is defined as {\it unit clauses .}
elements.sent-2411-066: The MaxSAT modulo theories (MaxSMT) problem is an optimization version of SMT, where clauses are divided into {\it hard clauses and {\it soft clauses with weight.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: MaxSMT aims to maximize the total weight of satisfied soft clauses while satisfying all hard clauses.
elements.sent-2411-068: Optimized Modulo theories (OMT) is an extension of SMT with an objective.
elements.sent-2411-069: OMT aims to find models that optimize given objectives while satisfying all constraints.
elements.sent-2411-070: The core SMT solving builds upon the strengths of SAT~\cite{kroening2016decision,ganzinger2004dpll , which contains the following Boolean automated reasoning techniques~\cite{biere2009handbook : {\it Unit Propagation , {\it Pure Literal Elimination , {\it Variable Elimination and {\it Subsumption Elimination etc. These techniques can efficiently handle the Boolean constraints and drastically simplify the SMT formula.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: However, in previous works, Boolean variables were not introduced to the constraint-based layout models, so the Boolean automated reasoning ability of SMT solvers was not fully utilized.
elements.sent-2411-072: Local search is an incomplete method that has recently been successfully applied to solve the SMT problem~\cite{frohlich2015stochastic,niemetz2016precise,niemetz2017propagation .}
elements.sent-2411-073: LocalSMT~\cite{cai2023local,cai2022local,li2023local is the state-of-the-art local search solver dedicated to SMT with the linear arithmetic theory.}
elements.sent-2411-074: It iteratively modifies the current solutions by applying the ``{\it critical move '' operator, which is appropriate for incremental scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, the {\it critical move operator assigns an arithmetic variable $x$ to the threshold value making constraint $\ell$ true, where $\ell$ is a falsified constraint containing $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Moreover, LocalSMT is a lightweight solver with a compact data structure, so it is suitable for scenarios with rigorous memory restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-077: As shown in Fig.~\ref{framework , to support real-time interaction of layouts even on terminal devices with limited computing resources, we propose to divide the workflow into two stages, namely the {\it development end and the {\it terminal end .}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Compared to the terminal end, the development end has sufficient computational resources, which can be utilized to ease the solving burden at the terminal end.
elements.sent-2411-079: By leveraging the abundant computational resources at the development end, two novel preprocessing methods, namely {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening and {\it Independent widget extraction , are proposed to simplify the formula and extract useful information in advance to enhance solving efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Subsequently, the preview module evaluates whether the layout meets expectations and notifies designers of potential issues.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the solving module, a two-level solving strategy is proposed to solve the constraints based on the current screen size, leveraging the reasoning and incremental solving power of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-082: In addition to complete SMT solvers such as Z3~\cite{moura2008z3 , a lightweight local search SMT solver is customized as the backend solver for this incremental scenario to improve the solving efficiency and reduce memory usage.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Finally, the solution is transferred into the layout for display.
elements.sent-2411-084: Note that, since the layouts of most practical web pages and mobile Apps support vertical scrolling, the impact of screen height on layout is minimal, and screen width becomes the primary factor influencing layout.
elements.sent-2411-085: Therefore, SMT-Layout focuses on the real-time interaction of real-world layouts in response to variations in screen width.
elements.sent-2411-086: This section describes the modeling module to formalize the layout based on MaxSMT.
elements.sent-2411-087: First, we define the basic properties of widgets, introducing the Boolean variable to illustrate the $visibility$ of widgets.
elements.sent-2411-088: Then, abundant preset layout containers are provided to specify real-world layouts, and a novel container called {\it Placeholder is proposed to support layout alternatives adapting to various screen sizes with one specification.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Besides numerical constraints illustrating the positional relationships, for the first time, Boolean constraints are applied to directly specify the hierarchical relationships among widgets as hard constraints.
elements.sent-2411-090: Based on these Boolean constraints, the reasoning capability of SMT solvers can be utilized to infer the visibility of widgets in advance, and the constraints regarding invisible widgets can be omitted to reduce the search space.
elements.sent-2411-091: Finally, soft constraints are introduced as Boolean variables to express the preference for alternative layouts.

elements.sent-2411-094: This Boolean property will be applied to illustrate the hierarchy relationships among widgets in the following context.
elements.sent-2411-095: If a widget is invisible, it will not be displayed and its corresponding constraints will not take effect.
elements.sent-2411-096: Given a widget $w$, these five properties are denoted as $[w]_w$, $[w]_h$, $[w]_x$, $[w]_y$ and $[w]_v$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-097: After solving the constraints, the solution consists of these properties, and visible widgets will be displayed according to their size and position properties.
elements.sent-2411-098: Abundant typical layout patterns, such as {\it Row , {\it Column , {\it Card , {\it Table , {\it Flow , {\it Waterfall , and {\it Flex are provided as preset containers to specify real-world layouts.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-121: Besides hard constraints including numerical constraints illustrating the positional relationship and Boolean constraints illustrating the hierarchy relationship, soft constraints are introduced to express preferences for alternative layouts.
elements.sent-2411-122: The soft constraints are in the form of Boolean variables with associated weights, and the goal is to maximize the total weight of satisfied soft constraints while satisfying all hard constraints.
elements.sent-2411-123: These soft constraints can be divided into the following 2 types: First, designers can flexibly define preferences for positional relationships among widgets, by introducing auxiliary Boolean variables as soft constraints.
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, the positional relationship is formalized as the conjunction of constraints.
elements.sent-2411-125: For example, the positional relationship that widgets $a$ and $b$ are center-aligned is formalized as $(2*[a]_x+[a]_w =2*_x+_w)\wedge(2*[a]_y+[a]_h =2*_y+_h) $.
elements.sent-2411-126: For each conjunction $F_{conj}$, the preference can be specified by introducing a fresh auxiliary Boolean variable $soft\_new$ and adding the following hard constraints: \begin{equation}soft\_new\rightarrow F_{conj}\quad \texttt{(if $soft\_new$ is true, $F_{conj}$ should be satisfied)}\end{equation} These auxiliary variables are set as soft constraints, indicating that they are preferred to be true, and consequently, the corresponding $F_{conj}$ are also preferred to be satisfied.

elements.sent-2411-132: After the modeling module, the specification of layouts is converted to MaxSMT formulas.
elements.sent-2411-133: However, solving MaxSMT formulas can be time-consuming in practice and cannot support real-time interaction at the terminal end.
elements.sent-2411-134: We leverage the abundant computational resources at the development end to enhance the solving efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-135: Hence, two novel preprocessing methods are proposed to simplify the original formula and extract useful information in advance to guide the search.
elements.sent-2411-136: We observe that the layout structure remains consistent within a specific interval of screen width, indicating that the truth assignments of soft constraints remain constant within such intervals.
elements.sent-2411-137: Furthermore, once the truth assignments of soft constraints are determined, the MaxSMT formula actually becomes the SMT formula.
elements.sent-2411-138: This conversion can improve solving efficiency, as SMT solving has made significant progress.
elements.sent-2411-139: Finally, once the $visibility$ properties of alternative widgets are determined, SMT solvers can automatically reason the $visibility$ of other widgets, depending on the Boolean constraints that illustrate the hierarchical information.

elements.sent-2411-141: Based on the above observation, a preprocessing method called {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening is proposed to fully utilize the efficient solving ability and automated reasoning ability of SMT solvers.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Given a size property $p$ and its related soft constraints, the method gradually traverses the range of $p$, determining the truth assignments of soft constraints corresponding to the interval of $p$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Their correspondence relationships are detected as constraints, which can be used to convert MaxSMT formulas into SMT formulas.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{ soft\_constraints\_hardening} \label{soft_harden_alg} \SetKwInOut{Input}{INPUT} \SetKwInOut{Output}{OUTPUT} $ and the size property $p$} \Output{$C\_relation$, the constraint formalizing the relationship between the intervals of $p$ and the corresponding truth assignments of soft constraints} find the maximum and minimum value of $p$, $max\_val$ and $min\_val$, by applying OMT solver to $F_{max}.hard$ \; $C\_relation:=(min\_val\le p\le max\_val);${\tcc {determine the range of $p$}} $upper\_bound:= max\_val$\; \While{$upper\_bound>min\_val$}{ find the solution $\alpha$ by applying MaxSMT solver to solve $F_{max}\wedge(p=upper\_bound)$\; $\alpha_{soft}:=$ the truth assignment of soft constraints related to $p$ at the current size in $\alpha$\; find $lower\_bound$, the minimum value of $p$ regarding $\alpha_{soft}$, by applying OMT solver to solve $F_{max}.hard \wedge (\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft}}\ell)$\; $C\_relation:= C\_relation\wedge((\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft}}\ell)\leftrightarrow (lower\_bound\le p\le upper\_bound))$\; {\tcc {formalize the correspondence relationship with constraint}} $upper\_bound := lower\_bound-1$ } {\bf return} $C\_relation$\;
elements.sent-2411-145: First, we determine the maximum and minimum values of the size property $p$, namely $max\_val$ and $min\_val$, by adopting the OMT solver to solve the hard constraints of the MaxSMT formula (Line 1).
elements.sent-2411-146: The size property is limited in the range of $[min\_val, max\_val]$ (Line 2).
elements.sent-2411-147: Then, we traverse the range of $p$ and divide it into intervals based on the assignment of soft constraints related to $p$, denoted as $\alpha_{soft}$ (Lines 3--9).
elements.sent-2411-148: The upper bound of the current interval, denoted as $upper\_bound$, is initialized as $max\_val$ (Line 3).
elements.sent-2411-149: The MaxSMT solver is employed to solve the MaxSMT formula attached with the constraint $(p = upper\_bound)$ to obtain the solution $\alpha$ (Line 5), which is used to extract $\alpha_{soft}$ at the current size (Line 6).
elements.sent-2411-150: Then we determine the minimum possible value of $p$ regarding $\alpha_{soft}$, denoted as $lower\_bound$.
elements.sent-2411-151: It is accomplished by applying the OMT solver to solve the hard constraints attached with $(\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft }\ell)$, indicating that all soft constraints are fixed to the truth value specified in $\alpha_{soft}$ (Line 7).}
elements.sent-2411-152: Their relationship is formalized as $(\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft }\ell)\leftrightarrow (lower\_bound\le w\le upper\_bound)$, indicating that the truth assignment of soft constraints is set as $\alpha_{soft}$ if and only if $p$ falls into this interval (Line 8).}

elements.sent-2411-154: Finally, the algorithm outputs the constraint $C\_relation$, representing the relationship between the truth assignments of soft constraints and their corresponding intervals of $p$ (Line 10).

elements.sent-2411-164: In the practical layout development process, the widgets are designed and assembled in a modular manner.
elements.sent-2411-165: Thus, the sub-layouts of certain widgets do not affect other widgets outside the sub-layouts, and they are considered as ``independent'' widgets.
elements.sent-2411-166: By extracting the constraints related to ``independent'' widgets, the original formula is divided into a simplified overall formula and multiple formulas specifying the sub-layouts of ``independent'' widgets.
elements.sent-2411-167: Compared to the original formulas, these formulas are smaller and can be solved more efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-168: The criteria for the ``independent'' widget is formally defined as follows: given a widget $w$, the set of clauses involving the properties of any widget in $[w]_{sub}$ is defined as the $sub\_constraints$ of $w$, denoted as $F_{sub (w)$, and $w$ is {\bf independent if each clause $c\in F_{sub (w)$ does not concern the properties of any widget $w'\not \in \{w\cup[w]_{sub}\}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: In other words, the $sub\_constraints$ of an independent widget does not affect any widget outside the sub-layout, and it only affects the sub-layout itself.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{ Independent widgets extraction} \label{inner_outer} \SetKwInOut{Input}{INPUT} \SetKwInOut{Output}{OUTPUT} $} \Output{the overall constraints in the outer layer $Outer\_constraint$, and the set of constraints for independent widgets in the inner layer $Inner\_constraints$} $Inner\_constraints:=\phi$\; Extract the $sub\_cosntraints$ of independent widgets from $F_{max}$\; \For{$w$ in independent widgets}{ $C_{width}:=$ soft\_constraints\_hardending($F_{sub}(w)$,$[w]_w$)\; $C_{height}:=$ soft\_constraints\_hardending($F_{sub}(w)$,$[w]_h$)\; {\tcc{detect the influence of $F_{sub}(w)$ on $[w]_w$ and $[w]_h$}} $C\_inner:=F_{sub}(w).hard\wedge C_{width}\wedge C_{height};$ {\tcc{convert $F_{sub}(w)$ to inner-layer SMT formula}} $Inner\_constraints:= Inner\_constraints\cup C\_inner$\; $F_{max}:= F_{max}\wedge C_{width}\wedge C_{height}$\; {\tcc{reflect the intrinsic impact of $F_{sub}(w)$ to the outer layer}} } $C_{out\_width}:=$ soft\_constraints\_hardending($F_{max}$,$[screen]_w$)\; $Outer\_constraint:= F_{max}.hard\wedge C_{out\_width}${\tcc{convert $F_{max}$ to outer-layer SMT formula}} {\bf Return} ($Outer\_constraint$, $Inner\_constraints$)\;
elements.sent-2411-170: The $sub\_constaints$ of independent widgets can be extracted from the original formula to solve independently.
elements.sent-2411-171: After the extraction, the original formula is divided into two layers: the {\bf outer layer illustrates the overall layout, composed of the original constraints without the $sub\_constaints$ of independent widgets; the {\bf inner layer consists of multiple formulas specifying the layout of independent widgets based on their $sub\_constraints$.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Such extraction can be viewed as omitting the internal details of the sub-layouts.
elements.sent-2411-173: However, directly extracting $sub\_constraints$ from the original formulas can lose the embedded intrinsic constraints on independent widgets.
elements.sent-2411-174: For example, $F_{sub (Wide\_bar)$ limits the lower bound of $[Wide\_bar]_w$, and simply extracting it from the outer layer will omit such constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Without such intrinsic constraints, $[Wide\_bar]_w$ can be assigned with a smaller value than its lower bound by solving the outer layer formulas, but the $F_{sub (Wide\_bar)$ will become unsatisfiable accordingly.}
elements.sent-2411-176: To avoid losing the intrinsic information embedded in the extracted constraints, the influence of $sub\_constraints$ on its corresponding independent widget should be detected and reflected in the outer-layer formula.
elements.sent-2411-177: We apply the {\it Interval-based soft clauses hardening preprocessing method to the extracted $sub\_constraints$ of independent widgets, detecting the intrinsic constraints of $sub\_constraints$ on their size properties.}
elements.sent-2411-178: These detected constraints are integrated into the outer-layer formula to reflect the impact of $sub\_constraints$.
elements.sent-2411-179: Note that, to avoid mutual influence between width and height, which complicates the range partitioning, we only choose independent widgets with irrelevant height and width properties.
elements.sent-2411-180: Moreover, to make the independent widgets efficient to solve, the number of clauses in the extracted $sub\_constraints$ of each independent widget is limited in the threshold $t=300$.
elements.sent-2411-181: Alg.~\ref{inner_outer describes the {\it Independent widgets extraction preprocessing method in detail.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Specifically, we first extract the $sub\_cosntraints$ of independent widgets from the original MaxSMT formula (Line 2).
elements.sent-2411-183: For each independent widget $w$, the intrinsic constraint of $F_{sub (w)$ on its width and height properties is detected as $C_{width}$ and $C_{height}$ by applying the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening method (Lines 4--5).}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Then the $sub\_cosntraints$ is converted to an inner-layer SMT formula $C_{inner}$ (Line 6), and inserted into the inner-layer constraint set $Inner\_constraints$ (Line 7).
elements.sent-2411-185: The intrinsic influence of $sub\_cosntraints$ on the independent widget is reflected in the outer layer (Line 8).
elements.sent-2411-186: Finally, the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening method is applied to the MaxSMT formula in the outer layer, converting it into an SMT formula $C_{out}$ (Lines 9--10).}
elements.sent-2411-187: The preprocessing method returns the overall constraints in the outer layer and the set of constraints specifying the sub-layout of independent widgets in the inner layer, both of which are converted into SMT formulas (Line 11).

elements.sent-2411-191: Note that the influence of $sub\_constraints$ on other properties can be omitted for the following reasons: the $visibility$ property can be dismissed because the corresponding $sub\_constraints$ of those invisible independent widgets do not need to be solved.
elements.sent-2411-192: Moreover, since the sub-layout can be translated, the positional properties of the independent widget $w$, namely $[w]_x$ and $[w]_y$, can be simplified as 0 in the extracted constraints.

elements.sent-2411-194: Before deploying these SMT formulas to the terminal end, it is essential to solve the converted formulas on the development end and preview the results to ensure that the layout meets the expectations.
elements.sent-2411-195: Moreover, the following factors may result in potential issues, which can be addressed in advance: First, the original MaxSMT formula specified by the designer may contain conflict hard clauses, which only take effect at certain sizes.
elements.sent-2411-196: In this scenario, the ``unsatisfiable core'' function of SMT solvers should be employed to identify the conflict constraint sets and notify the designers.
elements.sent-2411-197: Secondly, the predetermined truth assignment of the soft constraints may become infeasible at certain sizes.
elements.sent-2411-198: Specifically, given the truth assignments of soft constraints, denoted as $\alpha_{soft}$, the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening preprocess identifies the corresponding intervals by utilizing the OMT solver to determine their maximum and minimum values.}
elements.sent-2411-199: This is based on the assumption that the interval is continuous and that $\alpha_{soft}$ is feasible within the interval.
elements.sent-2411-200: However, although the probability is low, the feasible interval corresponding to $\alpha_{soft}$ values may be discontinuous, and the SMT formula becomes unsatisfiable when the size property $p$ takes the value $curr\_p$ within these gaps.
elements.sent-2411-201: In the second situation, the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening preprocess should be reapplied to determine the continuous feasible interval for $\alpha_{soft}$.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Specifically, it should be applied to the MaxSMT formula attached with the constraint $(\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft }\ell)\rightarrow (p>curr\_p)$, ensuring that $(p>curr\_p)$ is enforced when determining the lower bound corresponding to $\alpha_{soft}$.}
elements.sent-2411-203: Detailed descriptions of the preview process algorithm can be found in Appendix \ref{appendix_a .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Suppose the discontinuous feasible interval corresponding to $\alpha_{soft}$ is $[1000,1400]\cup[1600,2000]$.
elements.sent-2411-205: The {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening preprocess determines the minimum and maximum values of the size property $p$ as 1000 and 2000 respectively, and $[1000,2000]$ is set as the corresponding interval.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, the SMT formula becomes unsatisfiable at the size value $p=1500$.
elements.sent-2411-207: To find the continuous feasible interval, the preprocess is reapplied to the MaxSMT formula attached with $(\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft }\ell)\rightarrow (p>1500)$.}
elements.sent-2411-208: When determining the lower bound for $\alpha_{soft}$, the constraint $(p>1500)$ takes effect.
elements.sent-2411-209: Consequently, the continuous interval $[1600,2000]$ can be determined.
elements.sent-2411-210: After the preprocessing and previewing module, the simplified constraints are deployed to the terminal end.
elements.sent-2411-211: A two-level solving strategy is proposed to solve the pre-processed formulas according to the current screen size, leveraging the reasoning and incremental solving power of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-212: Based on the solving strategy, two SMT solvers are adopted as backend solvers:.
elements.sent-2411-213: A mainstream complete SMT solver named Z3~\cite{moura2008z3.}
elements.sent-2411-214: A lightweight local search SMT solver named LocalSMT~\cite{cai2023local , customized to this incremental scenario to improve the solving efficiency and decrease memory usage.}
elements.sent-2411-215: As the screen width varies, these SMT formulas are solved by {\it incrementally modifying the constraints on screen width or size properties of independent widgets.}
elements.sent-2411-216: After the {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening preprocessing procedure, the range of size properties is divided into multiple intervals, and the corresponding truth assignments of soft constraints are determined in each interval.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Although these Boolean assignments can be inferred from the constraints, we explicitly evaluate them according to the interval in which the current size property is located.
elements.sent-2411-218: These assignments are incorporated into the SMT formulas as unit clauses, prompting the SMT solvers to apply {\it Boolean automated reasoning based on the Boolean constraints of hierarchical information.}
elements.sent-2411-219: With the {\it reasoning power of SMT solvers, invisible containers can be identified and their related positional constraints can be excluded in advance, drastically reducing the formula scale.}
elements.sent-2411-220: The truth assignments of soft constraints are updated {\it incrementally when the current size property enters a new interval.}
elements.sent-2411-221: Since the assignments of soft constraints remain constant within an interval, the Boolean reasoning can be reused within the interval, and there is no need to redo it every time the size properties vary.
elements.sent-2411-222: The solving process is divided into two levels: {\it Abstraction and {\it Refinement , targeting the outer and inner layer formulas respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: In the {\it abstraction level, the outer layer constraints are solved, and the properties of independent widgets are determined.}
elements.sent-2411-224: In the {\it refinement level, the sub-layouts of those visible independent widgets are determined by solving their corresponding $sub\_constraints$, attached with their size properties.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Then these sub-layouts are translated according to the corresponding position properties of independent widgets.
elements.sent-2411-226: In the {\it abstraction level shown in Fig.~\ref{outer , the overall layout is determined by solving the outer-layer constraints, according to the assignment of soft constraints regarding the current screen width.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Then in the {\it refinement level shown in Fig.~\ref{refine , the size properties of the visible independent widgets, $Wide\_bar$ and $3\_col\_Table$, are transmitted to the inner-layer constraints, and their sub-layouts are determined and relocated according to the position properties.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: In addition to a mainstream complete SMT solver called Z3~\cite{moura2008z3 , a lightweight local search SMT solver called LocalSMT is customized as the backend solver for the following reasons: First, most constraints remain satisfied as the screen width varies.}
elements.sent-2411-229: In such an incremental scenario, LocalSMT can utilize local search to incrementally modify the current solution and efficiently find a new solution near the current one.
elements.sent-2411-230: Second, LocalSMT is a lightweight local search solver dedicated to SMT formulas with the arithmetic theory and has a compact data structure.
elements.sent-2411-231: Thus, it can save memory usage and is more suitable for the terminal end with rigorous memory restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-232: In contrast, complete SMT solvers with general purpose occupy much memory, as they typically support abundant theories and maintain complex context information.
elements.sent-2411-233: Nevertheless, after applying Boolean automated reasoning, the SMT formulas still contain numerous equations illustrating the positional relationship among widgets.
elements.sent-2411-234: LocalSMT can get stuck on these equations because its "critical move" operator cannot comprehensively investigate the intrinsic relationships among them.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, a simplification method called {\bf Unit equation elimination is proposed to customize LocalSMT for layout constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-236: This method explores the relationship among equations and eliminates redundant variables.
elements.sent-2411-237: The detailed description is as follows: Given an SMT formula, we first extract the equations in unit clauses, denoted as {\it unit equations , as a system of linear equations.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Then the Guass-Jordan elimination~\footnote{\url{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_elimination is employed to resolve these linear equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: If there are solutions in the form of $x=k$ or $y=k*x+b$, where $x$ and $y$ are variables, and $k$ and $b$ are parameters, these variables can be eliminated by substituting them with the corresponding expressions.
elements.sent-2411-240: After the elimination, additional {\it unit equations may be introduced for the following reasons: first, once the truth values of certain constraints are established by preset variables, new unit clauses may be introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Secondly, certain inequalities will combine with other inequalities to generate new equations after the elimination.
elements.sent-2411-242: A typical situation is illustrated in the following example:.
elements.sent-2411-243: The variable $z$ is set to 0 after the {\it Unit equation elimination .}
elements.sent-2411-244: The clause $(x+y=0)\vee(z>0)$ is converted to a {\it unit equation , as $z>0$ has already been established as false.}
elements.sent-2411-245: The clauses $(a+b+z\ge0)$ and $(a+b\le0)$ are converted to a new {\it unit equation $(a+b=0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Thus, the {\it Unit equation elimination procedure outlined above should be repeated until no more variables can be eliminated.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Based on the proposed strategies, we implement SMT-Layout and evaluate it on multiple benchmarks derived from real-world layouts.
elements.sent-2411-248: These specifications are deployed to two terminal devices to evaluate the performance: a laptop with an Intel i7 CPU, and a mobile phone with a Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 CPU.
elements.sent-2411-249: The solving efficiency is crucial to facilitate the real-time interaction of real-world layouts as the screen width varies.
elements.sent-2411-250: Moreover, memory usage is also a critical metric for measurement, since the terminal equipment typically has rigorous memory usage restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-251: By traversing the range of screen width for 10 runs, we measure the average interaction time, maximum interaction time, and memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-252: Note that the run time is measured in milliseconds while memory usage is measured in MB.
elements.sent-2411-253: To save space, in the following context, the interaction time is presented in the form of Average (Maximum).

elements.sent-2411-254: The reasons are as follows: First, SMT-Layout is dedicated to real-world layouts, and the benchmarks are derived from actual GUIs.
elements.sent-2411-255: However, ORCLayout mainly focuses on the Flow layout pattern and does not support abundant layout containers to specify these real-world layouts.
elements.sent-2411-256: Specifically, We have consulted the authors of ORCLayout, and they admit that ORCLayout is unsuitable for practical layout design because the widgets in ORCLayout are compactly adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-257: Second, ORCSolver has internal bugs and frequently crashes during solving, preventing it from being executed in comparison experiments.
elements.sent-2411-258: Specifically, we have downloaded the source code from their repository~\footnote{https://github.com/YueJiang-nj/ORCSolver-CHI2020 , and we find that ORCSolver crashes even on almost all original examples~\footnote{We tried all original examples in the directory ``/ORCSolver-CHI2020/Code/ORCSolver (Ours)''.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: We find that all examples encounter the error ``std::out\_of\_range'' when calling the embedded CVXPY solver, with only one exception ``flow\_around\_pattern.py''.. .
elements.sent-2411-260: As a reference, according to the results reported in their paper, the solving time of ORCsolver on benchmarks of a similar scale is several hundred milliseconds, which is considerably slower than our approach.
elements.sent-2411-261: Even on the benchmark of the smallest scale, consisting of only 20 widgets, the average reported interaction time reaches up to 149 milliseconds, unable to meet the requirement for real-time interaction.

elements.sent-2411-307: Although these layout models are intuitive, they have the following limitations: First, widgets cannot be aligned across a hierarchy~\cite{lutteroth2008modular .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Moreover, these models often implicitly add more constraints than desired, which makes them hard to maintain~\cite{zeidler2012comparing, lutteroth2006user .}
elements.sent-2411-309: To overcome these shortcomings, more modern layout models use constraints to specify the layout, employing various constraint types and both explicit and implicit constraint specifications.
elements.sent-2411-310: Linear constraint-based layouts have the following advantages: First, linear constraint systems have a well-understood mathematical foundation, which makes the results less dependent on implementation details, and thus it is easy to maintain~\cite{zeidler2012comparing,lutteroth2006user .}
elements.sent-2411-311: Moreover, it can align widgets across hierarchies, which is impossible in conventional layout models.
elements.sent-2411-312: Furthermore, linear layout systems can be combined to enable modular reuse of existing layout specifications~\cite{lutteroth2008modular .}
elements.sent-2411-313: Finally, most conventional layout models can be converted to linear constraint systems~\cite{weber2010reduction,zeidler2017tiling .}
elements.sent-2411-314: Although linear constraint-based layouts are flexible, linear constraint-based layout models share the same drawback as the conventional layout models: multiple layout alternatives need to be specified when the page size varies significantly, and the layout manager determines which layout to apply at runtime based on contexts such as screen size or aspect ratio.

elements.sent-2411-332: In contrast, SMT-Layout goes further and has the following advantages: First, the ORCSolver does not support Boolean variables and cannot directly illustrate the hierarchical relationships by Boolean constraints.

elements.sent-2411-337: In SMT-Layout, SMT solvers are applied incrementally in our solving module, and LocalSMT is tailored for such incremental scenarios by modifying the current solution.
elements.sent-2411-338: Moreover, our solving module can achieve real-time response at the millisecond level, even on terminal equipment with weak computational power.
elements.sent-2411-339: Furthermore, ORC solvers employ specific modular heuristics tailored to each layout pattern, rather than relying on a general approach.
elements.sent-2411-340: New heuristics should be designed to adapt to new or more complex layout patterns.
elements.sent-2411-341: In contrast, our method is general and does not rely on specific layout patterns, allowing for the flexible enrichment of layout patterns without additional adaptation, and it can enjoy further progress in SMT solving.
elements.sent-2411-342: In this paper, by fully utilizing the flexible expressive ability of (Max)SMT and the powerful solving ability of SMT solvers, we propose a novel layout approach named SMT-Layout.
elements.sent-2411-343: SMT-Layout is the first practical constraint-based layout model that can support real-time interaction for real-world layout adapting to various screen sizes with only one specification.
elements.sent-2411-344: SMT-Layout introduces Boolean variables to encode the hierarchical information among widgets, boosting the reasoning ability of SMT solvers.
elements.sent-2411-345: By utilizing the abundant computing resources at the development end, two novel preprocessing methods are proposed to simplify the constraints and extract useful information to ease the solving burden at the terminal end.
elements.sent-2411-346: After deploying the preprocessed constraints, SMT solvers, including a customized local search solver, are employed as backend solvers to solve them incrementally, leveraging their reasoning ability.
elements.sent-2411-347: Experimental results show the efficiency of our approach, even on equipment with weak computational power and rigorous memory limitation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Ablation experiments are conducted to confirm the effectiveness of the proposed strategies.

elements.sent-2411-424: Given a preprocessed SMT formula and its original MaxSMT formula, we traverse the range of size property $p$ from the maximum value $max\_val$ to the minimum value $min\_val$ (Lines 2--12).
elements.sent-2411-425: We first apply SMT solvers to solve the SMT formula by specifying the size property $p$ to the current value $curr\_p$ (Line 3).
elements.sent-2411-426: Suppose the SMT formula is unsatisfiable at the current size property value (Line 4), the SMT solver is employed to solve the hard constraints of the original MaxSMT formula at the current size property value (Line 5).
elements.sent-2411-427: If the hard constraints remain unsatisfiable (Line 6), indicating that the original specification contains conflict constraints at the current size, the ``unsatisfiable core'' function is performed to identify a conflicting constraint set and notify the designer to modify the specification (Line 7).
elements.sent-2411-428: Otherwise, the current truth assignment of soft constraints is infeasible at the current size, indicating that the corresponding interval is discontinuous.
elements.sent-2411-429: We extract the truth assignment of soft constraints related to the current value of the size property, denoted as $\alpha_{soft}$ (Line 9).
elements.sent-2411-430: The {\it Interval-based soft constraints hardening procedure should be reapplied to determine the continuous interval corresponding to $\alpha_{soft}$ (Line 10).}
elements.sent-2411-431: The MaxSMT formula is converted to a new SMT formula $F$ accordingly (Line 11).
elements.sent-2411-432: After the traversing, the new SMT formula without conflict is returned (Line 13).
elements.sent-2411-433: Since the overall SMT formula in the outer layer is based on the constraints of independent widgets, we first preview the SMT formulas in the inner layer.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{ Previewing process} \label{preview} \SetKwInOut{Input}{INPUT} \SetKwInOut{Output}{OUTPUT} $, and the size property $p$} \Output{The conflict constraint sets or an SMT formula without conflict} $curr\_p:=max\_val$\; \While{$curr\_p\ge min\_val$}{ $SMT\_result:=$ apply SMT solver to solve $F\wedge(p==curr\_p)$\; \If{$SMT\_result==unsat$}{ {\tcc{$F$ contains conflict constraints at the current size}} $hard\_result:=$ apply SMT solver to solve $F_{max}.hard\wedge (p==curr\_p)$\; \If{$hard\_result==unsat$}{ {\tcc{the original specification contains conflict hard constraints}} {\bf return} the ``unsatisfiable core'' found by SMT solver\; } \Else{ {\tcc{the interval corresponding to the current truth assignment of soft constraints is discontinuous}} $\alpha_{soft}:=$ the assignment of soft constraints\; $C\_new:=$ soft\_constraints\_hardening($F_{max}\wedge((\bigwedge_{\ell\in \alpha_{soft}}\ell)\rightarrow (p>curr\_p)),p$)\; $F:=F_{max}.hard\wedge C\_new$\; } } $curr\_p:= curr\_p-1$\; } {\bf return} $F$\;
